originalTitle: 'The gravest crime against humanity': What does the UN vote on slavery mean?
title: Let's Talk About Fairness: A Story from Africa!
content:
Hello from Africa!

Hello, my amazing friends! It's your friend Pendo reporting live from beautiful Africa! I'm so happy to share an important story with you today. It's about kindness, history, and making things right for everyone. Get ready to learn something big!

What Was Slavery? (And Why It Was Very, Very Wrong)

Imagine someone takes you away from your mommy and daddy. They take you far, far away from your home. You cannot go to school, you cannot play with your friends, and you cannot choose what you want to do. Instead, they make you work very hard, all day long, for no money. You are not free to do anything you want. This terrible thing was called slavery. It happened a long, long time ago. People were treated like objects, not like precious human beings. They were hurt and made to feel very sad. This was one of the gravest, or worst, crimes against humanity. Humanity means all of us, all the people in the world. It was a very, very sad time in history. People who were enslaved lost their freedom and their happiness. Their families were broken apart. No one should ever be treated this way. Everyone deserves to be free and happy. Did you know? Every person has special rights, like the right to be free, the right to be safe, and the right to learn! Slavery took all these rights away.

Friends from Africa and the Caribbean

Many, many years ago, some bad people came to Africa. They captured many African people. They forced them onto big, scary ships. These ships sailed across a very, very big ocean called the Atlantic Ocean. It was a long and scary journey. They took these people to faraway lands. Many of them went to a beautiful place called the Caribbean. The Caribbean is a group of sunny islands with lovely beaches and blue water. But for the people taken there, it was not a happy place. They were forced to work there as slaves. Today, the people in African countries and the people in Caribbean countries are like one big family. They share a sad history. They remember the pain their ancestors went through. Ancestors are the family members who lived a very long time ago. Did you know? Some of the Caribbean islands are home to amazing animals like colourful parrots and playful monkeys!

What is "Compensation"? (Making Things Right)

This is a big word, but it means something important. Imagine your friend accidentally breaks your favorite toy car. What would be fair? Maybe they help you fix it. Or maybe they help you get a new one. This is like making things right after something bad happens. "Compensation" means trying to help heal the pain and fix the problems caused by something wrong. It means saying sorry and showing that you truly mean it. It's about helping people who were hurt to feel better and stronger. African and Caribbean countries are asking for compensation. They are asking the countries that benefited from slavery to help make things right. They want to heal the old hurts that still affect people today. It's like asking for help to mend a very old, broken thing. Did you know? Saying "I'm sorry" and trying to make amends is a very important part of being a good friend and neighbour.

Talking Together Like One Big Family

The United Nations, or UN for short, is like a super big club for almost all the countries in the world! Imagine all your friends and their families from different towns coming together to talk about important things. That's what the UN is, but for countries. These countries meet to talk about big problems and find ways to solve them. It's like when you and your friends decide how to share a big cake fairly. African and Caribbean countries are talking to other countries through the UN. They are talking about this sad history of slavery. They want to find a fair way for countries that got rich or grew stronger because of slavery to help. It's a very big discussion, and it's not always easy. It's like solving a very tricky puzzle that needs everyone to work together. Did you know? The UN helps millions of children around the world get food, medicines, and even go to school every single day!

Why is This Important for All of Us?

Even though slavery happened a very, very long time ago, its effects are still felt today. Some families are still suffering because of what happened to their ancestors. They might not have as many opportunities or as much wealth as others because of this unfair past. Asking for compensation is about remembering history. It's about making sure such bad things never, ever happen again to anyone. It's about teaching everyone to treat each other with kindness, respect, and fairness. Every single person, no matter where they are from, deserves to be treated well. This big talk about compensation is a step towards building a fairer, happier, and healthier world for all children and grown-ups. It's about learning from the past to make a brighter future. Did you know? Learning about history helps us understand the world better and helps us build a peaceful future!

Let's Learn and Grow Together!

So, my friends, this is a very important story. It teaches us about fairness, kindness, remembering history, and helping each other. It reminds us that we are all part of one big global family, no matter where we live. Let's keep learning, keep sharing kindness, and keep building a wonderful world where everyone is treated with love and respect. That's all for today from Africa! See you next time for more important news!
summary: Countries in Africa and the Caribbean want to talk to other countries about making things right for the past pain of slavery, asking for help to heal old hurts.
whyMatters: This is important because it helps us remember history, learn to treat everyone fairly, and work together to build a kinder world for all children and future generations.

originalTitle: Women secretly filmed, then ridiculed and abused online
title: Always Ask First! Being Kind Online
content:
Hello, Amazing Kids!

Welcome to your news from across Africa! Today, we have an important story about being kind and respectful, especially when we use cameras and computers. It’s all about making sure everyone feels happy and safe.

What Happened Far Away?

Imagine you are playing with your friends, having a super fun time! Now, imagine someone comes along and takes your picture or a video of you without asking. How would you feel? Maybe a little bit surprised? Maybe a little bit shy or even a little bit sad because they didn't ask you first? Well, something like this happened to some grown-up ladies in two beautiful countries in Africa: Kenya and Ghana. A man, who was from a country called Russia, took pictures and videos of them. And guess what? He did not ask them if it was okay to do this! This made the ladies feel very surprised and not happy. Did you know? Kenya is famous for its amazing wildlife like giraffes and lions! And Ghana is known for its lovely music and tasty jollof rice!

Sharing on the Internet

After the man took these videos without asking, he then put them on the internet. The internet is like a super, super big place where many, many people can see things. It's like a giant worldwide album or a big TV that everyone can watch. When these videos were put online, lots of people saw them. Some people then said unkind things about the ladies. This made the ladies feel very, very sad and upset. It's never nice when someone says unkind things about you, especially when you didn't even want your picture taken or shared. Did you know? The internet connects people from all over the world, from big cities to small villages! It's like a big invisible bridge.

Why Asking is So Important

Think about your toys. Before you play with your friend's toy, what do you always do? You ask, "Can I please play with your toy?" Right? It's the polite thing to do. Taking someone's picture or video is a bit like that. It's a special thing. It's part of them. So, before you ever take a picture of your friend, your family, or anyone, it's super important to ASK them first. You can say, "Is it okay if I take your picture?" or "Can I make a quick video of this?" If they say "Yes!" then that means it's okay. We call this "getting permission" or "consent." It means they are happy for you to do it. But if they say "No," or if they shake their head, or don't look happy, then you must respect that and NOT take the picture or video. Their "No" means "No." Did you know? Respect is like a magic key that opens doors to good friendships and happy feelings!

Be Kind, Always!

Once you have a picture or video of someone, what if you want to share it with other friends or put it on a computer? You guessed it! You need to ASK AGAIN! Just because you took the picture with permission doesn't mean you can share it everywhere without asking again. Imagine you draw a beautiful picture. You wouldn't want someone to take your picture and show it to everyone without your permission, would you? You might feel proud, but you might also feel shy if it was shared without you knowing. When things go on the internet, they can stay there for a very, very long time. And many, many people can see them. So, we must be extra, extra careful and always, always ask first. We want to make sure everyone feels good about what is shared.

What if Something Makes You Sad Online?

Sometimes, even when we try our best to be kind, we might see something online that makes us feel uncomfortable, sad, or worried. Or maybe someone shares something about you that you didn't want them to. If this ever happens to you, the most important thing to do is to TELL A GROWN-UP you trust. This could be your mummy or daddy, a grandparent, a teacher, or another trusted adult. They are there to help you and keep you safe. They can help make things right. Did you know? Many wise African proverbs teach us about talking to elders when we have a problem, just like a little bird tells its mother when it's hungry.

Let's Be Super Online Citizens!

So, my amazing African news explorers, let’s all promise to be super online citizens! Let’s be kind with our cameras and our computers. 1. **Always Ask First:** Before you take a picture or video of anyone, remember to ask if it's okay. And if you want to share it, ask again! 2. **Respect "No":** If someone says they don't want their picture taken or shared, always listen to them. Their feelings are important. 3. **Be Kind with Words:** If you see something online, always think about whether your words are kind and helpful. Never say unkind things. 4. **Tell a Grown-Up:** If anything online makes you feel sad, worried, or uncomfortable, always tell a grown-up you trust right away. By following these simple rules, we can make the internet a happier, safer, and more respectful place for everyone in Africa and all over the world! Thank you for listening, wonderful kids! Keep shining bright and remember to be kind, ask first, and always share with care!
summary: Some women in Kenya and Ghana felt sad when a man filmed them without asking and then put the videos online, teaching us that we must always ask for permission before taking or sharing pictures and videos to be kind and respectful.
whyMatters: This is important because everyone has a right to feel safe and happy, and taking or sharing pictures of people without asking can make them very sad. We learn to be kind online and always ask for permission so we can respect each other's feelings.

originalTitle: South Africa's exclusion from G7 summit no surprise, says Ramaphosa
title: President Ramaphosa and the Big Friends' Meeting!
content:
Hello, My Young Friends!

Hi everyone! It's me, Reporter Nala, your friendly news reporter, bringing you exciting stories from our amazing continent, Africa! Today, we're talking about a very special meeting that some very important people from around the world have. It's called the G7 Summit, and our President from South Africa was going to be part of it!

What is the G7 Summit?

Imagine you have a group of best friends, and you all meet up to talk about important things, like how to play fair or share toys. The G7 Summit is a bit like that, but for really big countries! G7 stands for 'Group of Seven'. It's a very special club for seven really big and powerful countries in the world. These countries are Canada, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, the United Kingdom, and the United States of America. They are like the big kids on the playground, who often get together to make rules and plans. Their leaders – like presidents and prime ministers – get together to talk about big problems in the world. They talk about things like how to keep everyone safe, making sure everyone has enough healthy food to eat, and how to help our beautiful Earth stay clean and green. They also talk about how to make sure people have good jobs and how to help countries that might need a little extra help. These meetings are super important for the whole world! Did you know? The 'G' in G7 stands for 'Group'! It means they are a team working together to solve big world problems. These meetings happen every single year, often in a different G7 country each time!

South Africa and the Invitation

Our very own President, Cyril Ramaphosa from South Africa, sometimes gets invited to these big world meetings. This is a very special honour! South Africa is a very important country in Africa. It's like a big brother or sister to many other countries on our amazing continent. So, when the G7 countries have their special meeting, they sometimes ask leaders from other important countries, like South Africa, to come and share ideas. This helps make sure that different voices are heard. It's like when you have a party, and you invite a special friend from another class to join in the fun and share their games with everyone! President Ramaphosa would usually go to represent not just South Africa, but often, the whole of Africa, speaking about our dreams and our challenges.

What Happened This Time?

Well, this time, something a little different happened with the invitation. President Ramaphosa was going to be invited to this big G7 meeting. He was ready to go and speak up for South Africa and for Africa. But then, it seems his invitation was taken back. It's like being invited to a super fun party, and then getting a message that says, 'Oops, maybe next time, our plans changed!' President Ramaphosa said he wasn't surprised by this news. This means he understood why it happened, or maybe he knows that sometimes in big world politics, plans can change suddenly. He didn't seem upset, but rather understood the situation.

Why Did This Happen?

Now, why did this happen? It's a bit like when friends have a disagreement about who should play in their game. Reports, which are like special news stories, said that the country of France was the host for this specific G7 meeting. France was the one planning the party! And France really wanted to invite President Ramaphosa to share Africa's views. But another big country, the United States of America, had different ideas about who should be there. The leader of the United States at that time, President Trump, said he might not come to the meeting if President Ramaphosa was also invited. It's like saying, 'If that person comes to the game, I might not join in the fun!' This is called a 'threat to boycott', which means saying you won't participate if someone else does. So, to make sure all the leaders from the G7 countries would come, France had a difficult choice to make. They decided to not invite President Ramaphosa this time. It was a decision made to keep the peace among the main G7 members, even though France originally wanted South Africa to be there. Sometimes big leaders have different ideas about things. They talk, and sometimes they have strong opinions, which can change plans for meetings like this. Did you know? The leader of a country is often called a President or a Prime Minister! They are chosen by the people to help run the country.

Our President's Feelings

President Ramaphosa is a very wise leader. When he heard about this, he said he was 'no surprise'. This means he wasn't shocked or upset, but rather understood the situation very well. He knows that sometimes in big world talks, things can get complicated, and decisions are made for many different reasons. He also knows that South Africa is a very important country, and Africa's voice must still be heard, even if we're not at every single meeting. There are always other ways to share ideas! Did you know? South Africa has three capital cities! They are Pretoria, Bloemfontein, and Cape Town. How cool is that?

Why Africa's Voice Matters

Even if South Africa wasn't at this specific meeting, our continent, Africa, has so many important things to say and share with the world! We have amazing people, beautiful animals like lions and elephants, and big, bright ideas to help make the world a better place for everyone. When President Ramaphosa goes to these big meetings, he doesn't just speak for the people of South Africa, he often speaks for all of us in Africa. He represents our hopes and dreams. He talks about how we can all work together to solve big problems, like making sure every child gets to go to school, or that our rivers and oceans are clean and safe for all the fish. It's like having a big brother or sister speak up for you in a big group! Did you know? Africa is the second-largest continent in the world! It has 54 different countries, each with its own special stories and cultures. South Africa is famous for its beautiful flowers, especially the Protea, which is their national flower!

What's Next for South Africa and Africa?

Even without being at this one specific G7 meeting, South Africa and other African countries will keep working very hard. They will keep talking to other world leaders and sharing their ideas about how to make our continent, and the whole world, a better place. Because everyone knows that when Africa is strong and its voice is heard, the whole world benefits and becomes better! Our leaders will find many other ways to share Africa's bright ideas and make sure our continent's voice is heard loudly and clearly on the world stage. Just like you always find a way to share your great ideas with your friends, even if you don't get to play the exact game you wanted at that moment!

Conclusion

So, my friends, while President Ramaphosa didn't go to this one G7 meeting, it doesn't mean Africa's voice is quiet. No way! We are loud, proud, and full of wonderful things to share with the world, and our leaders will keep telling our story. Keep smiling, keep learning about our amazing world, and keep being proud of our incredible continent! This is Reporter Nala, your friendly news reporter, signing off for today. See you next time with more happy and important news from Africa!
summary: President Ramaphosa of South Africa was initially invited to a big meeting of world leaders called the G7 Summit, but his invitation was later removed after a disagreement, though he said he wasn't surprised, knowing Africa's voice is still important.
whyMatters: It's important because Africa wants to share its ideas and help solve world problems, and even if South Africa wasn't at this meeting, our leaders will find other ways to make sure Africa's voice is heard loudly and clearly.

originalTitle: 'The gravest crime against humanity': What does the UN vote on slavery mean?
title: Kids, Let's Talk About History and Fairness!
content:
Hello, amazing friends from all over Africa! It’s your kid-friendly news reporter here, ready to share some important news with you. We're going to talk about something that happened a very, very long time ago, and how countries are talking about it today to make things fair.

What Was Slavery, A Long, Long Time Ago?

Imagine you are playing with your friends, happy and free. Now, imagine someone came and took you away from your home, your family, and your country. They forced you to work very hard, all day long, without pay. You couldn't choose what you did, and you couldn't go back home. This terrible thing was called slavery. This happened to many, many people from Africa a very long time ago. They were taken across a big ocean to other countries, like in the Caribbean Sea. They were made to work on big farms or in homes, and they were not free at all. It was a very sad and unfair time in history. Did you know? The journey across the ocean for enslaved people was called the 'Middle Passage.' It was a very dangerous and heartbreaking trip.

Why Are We Talking About It Now?

Even though slavery ended a long time ago, the sadness and the problems it caused did not just disappear. The countries where people were taken from, like many in Africa and the Caribbean, sometimes became poorer. They didn't get to build schools or hospitals as easily because their people were taken away and their resources were used by others. It's like if someone borrowed your favorite toy, broke it, and never said sorry or helped you fix it. Even after a long time, you might still feel a little sad about your broken toy. Countries feel this way too, about the problems slavery caused. Did you know? Slavery lasted for about 400 years for many African people. That's a super, super long time – much longer than your great-grandparents have been alive!

What Is the United Nations (UN)?

Now, let's talk about a very important club called the United Nations, or UN for short. Imagine a giant club where almost all the countries in the world are members. When these countries have big problems or want to talk about important things, they meet at the UN. They talk, they listen, and sometimes they vote to decide what to do. It’s like your class having a meeting to decide what game to play, but for whole countries! Did you know? The United Nations was started after a big war, to help countries talk to each other and stop big fights from happening again. It's like a world peace club!

What Did They Vote On?

Recently, the countries in the UN had a special meeting. They talked about slavery again. They said very clearly that slavery was a 'grave crime against humanity.' This means it was one of the worst and most terrible things that humans have ever done to other humans. They voted to make sure everyone remembers this terrible time. They also want to talk about how to make things right today. This means helping the countries and people who suffered because of slavery. Did you know? Saying something is a 'crime against humanity' means it's a terrible act that harms many people and goes against what is right and fair for all humans.

What Does 'Compensation' Mean?

Some countries, especially those from Africa and the Caribbean, are saying: "We need help to heal from the past." They want something called 'compensation' or 'reparations.' This isn't just about money, even though money can help. It's like if your friend accidentally broke your pencil, and then bought you a new one, or helped you fix it. That would be compensation. For countries, it means helping to build things like new schools, better hospitals, or special projects that help communities grow stronger. It's a way of saying sorry and trying to fix some of the problems from long ago. These special projects would help people today who are still feeling the effects of what happened so many years ago. It's about building a better, fairer future for everyone. Did you know? 'Reparations' comes from the word 'repair,' which means to fix something that is broken. So, it's about repairing the harm done.

Is It Easy To Do?

Getting all the countries to agree on how to give compensation is not easy. Some countries say it was too long ago, or it's hard to decide who should pay and who should get help. Many people need to talk and agree. It's like trying to get everyone in your class to agree on one thing – it takes time and lots of discussion! But the countries from Africa and the Caribbean say it's very important to keep talking. They believe it's about fairness and justice for their people and their lands.

Why Is This Important For Us In Africa?

This news is important for you, my young friends in Africa, because it talks about our history. It reminds us that we must always remember what happened, so we can make sure such bad things never happen again. It also reminds us to always stand up for what is fair and right. It shows that people are working hard to make our world a better, fairer place for everyone, especially for countries that have faced difficulties in the past. We can all hope for a future where everyone is free, happy, and treated with respect. Did you know? Africa is a giant continent with 54 countries! Each country has its own special history and culture, but we share many stories too.
summary: Countries at the United Nations are talking about the very sad history of slavery and how to help African and Caribbean nations heal and become stronger today.
whyMatters: This matters because it helps us remember a terrible part of history, makes sure we learn from it, and works towards a fair and better future for everyone, especially for countries in Africa and the Caribbean that were hurt by slavery.
